nativeRange.habitat: Rathbun's Tetra is native to slow-moving streams, tributaries, and floodplain habitats within the Rio Paraguay, Rio Paraná, and Rio Uruguay drainages. In the wild it favors areas shaded by floating or overhanging vegetation, with leaf litter and submerged roots providing cover, and is subject to significant seasonal variation in water parameters.

decorPreferences: dense planting with fine-leaved plants (Java moss, hornwort, water sprite)

diet.notes: Unselective micro-predator that accepts most aquarium foods. High-quality flake, micro-pellets, and granules form a solid staple diet. Live and frozen foods (brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworms, tubifex, mosquito larvae) should be offered regularly to bring out best coloration and condition. Feed small portions 2–3 times daily.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetra, neon tetra, cardinal tetra)
avoidWith: long-finned varieties (fancy guppies, bettas, angelfish) — fin-nipping risk
very small nano fish that may be intimidated

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Dirk-Peter Gartner, 640x480. Live, vividly coloured adult clearly showing the diagnostic emerald-green iridescent body and vivid red flush on the lower flank/fins. Only CC-friendly live photograph available; modest resolution and held-in-hand setting, single specimen (schooling species).

summary: Rathbun’s Tetra is a small, active South American schooling fish reaching 4–5 cm, prized for its emerald-green body and vivid red flush along the lower flank. Notably cool-tolerant down to 18°C, it is well suited to unheated subtropical setups and community tanks. Groups of six or more are recommended.
careNotes: Rathbun’s Tetra is endemic to the Rio Paraguay, Rio Paraná, and Rio Uruguay drainages across Paraguay, Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay. The older scientific name Aphyocharax rathbuni remains widely used in the aquarium trade, and the species is sometimes confused with related Hyphessobrycon tetras due to superficial color similarities and overlapping common names, though Aphyocharax belongs to a distinct subfamily within the family Characidae. A minimum group of six is essential — solitary or paired specimens tend to become stressed and display noticeably poorer coloration. Rathbun’s Tetra is notably cool-tolerant, thriving down to 18°C, making it one of the few tetras broadly considered suitable for subtropical unheated aquariums in temperate climates. A planted aquarium with a dark substrate and floating plants to diffuse overhead light brings out the iridescent green and red coloration to best effect. Although generally peaceful, Rathbun’s Tetra has a reputation for fin-nipping, particularly toward long-finned or slow-moving tankmates; robust, similarly sized community fish are preferable choices. The species is broadly considered adaptable to a wide range of water chemistry within the stated parameters, and water quality should be maintained with regular partial water changes. Coloration in juveniles is subdued — full adult coloration develops over approximately 16 months. In mature males, the tips of the dorsal, anal, and pelvic fins become white; females are typically fuller-bodied.
breedingNotes: Rathbun's Tetra is an egg scatterer that spawns readily in groups when given appropriate conditions. A separate 40–60 L breeding tank fitted with fine-leaved plants such as Java moss or hornwort, a spawning mop, or sterilized marbles spread across the base will help protect eggs from being eaten by the adults. Slightly soft water (gH 2–8), a pH around 6.5, and a gentle temperature increase to around 25–26°C tends to trigger spawning, which typically occurs at dawn. The dominant male and the largest female account for most of the egg deposition, and up to 500 eggs may be produced in a single spawn. Adults should be removed immediately after spawning to prevent egg predation. Eggs hatch in 2–3 days and fry become free-swimming within 4–6 days. Initial feeds of infusoria or a commercial liquid fry food can be progressed to freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii as the fry grow. No specialized conditioning is required beyond a clean, well-planted setup.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aphyocharax rathbuni
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Paraguay, Paraná, Uruguay drainages; Brazil, Paraguay, Argentina, Uruguay), temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 36–357 ppm, adult size 35–45 mm SL, habitat description, behavior (fin-nipper, schooling), diet, and breeding notes (egg scatterer, up to 500 eggs, hatch 2–3 days).

additional.name: FishBase — Aphyocharax rathbuni
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Characidae, Aphyocharacinae, Characiformes), maximum size (7.1 cm SL morphological max; aquarium specimens typically 4–5 cm), native range (Paraguay River basin), IUCN Least Concern status, and named after Dr. Richard Rathbun (1852–1918).

additional.name: AquaInfo.nl — Aphyocharax rathbuni Green Fire Tetra
additional.notes: Key source for cool-tolerance lower bound of 18°C; confirmed breeding temperature 25.5°C, synonyms (Aphyocharax stramineus, Piabarchus stramineus), GH 8–12, native range includes Rio Paraguay and Rio Guaporé, schooling group of ~12 recommended for best display.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Rathbun’s Bloodfin Tetra
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (tributaries of Rio Paraguay in Paraguay), water parameters (20–26°C, pH 6.0–7.0, dH up to 18), adult size 4 cm, peaceful temperament, fin-nipping risk with long-finned fish, omnivorous diet, and egg-scattering breeding behavior.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Green Fire Tetra
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 4–5 cm, minimum tank 60 L, temperature 20–26°C, pH 6–7.5, hardness 8–20°N, schooling behavior, omnivorous diet, egg-scattering breeding requiring fine-leaved plants, lifespan 3–6 years.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterers that spawn in groups in a planted tank furnished with fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop. Adults should be removed after spawning to prevent egg predation. Up to 500 eggs may be produced per spawn; eggs hatch in 2–3 days and fry become free-swimming in 4–6 days. Begin feeding with infusoria or liquid fry food, then progress to brine shrimp nauplii.
